results.query: Does the range increase from a Sniper's Scope apply to the range of Area Fire or Auto‑Fire attacks in Starfinder 2e?
results.reference_answer: The Sniper’s Scope weapon upgrade increases the range increment of the weapon it is attached to but explicitly notes that this range increase does not change the size of area attacks【915641794802063†L72-L83】.  Area Fire and Auto‑Fire are actions with the area and attack traits.  When you use Area Fire or Auto‑Fire you don’t make an attack roll; instead, each creature in the area attempts a basic Reflex save against your class DC plus the weapon’s tracking value【537749994614211†L66-L72】【591629063671977†L66-L71】.  Because the scope’s benefit only increases the weapon’s range increment and explicitly excludes increasing the area, it has no effect on Area Fire or Auto‑Fire actions.

results.query: In Starfinder 2e, how can a character gain their deity’s favored weapon and other devotee benefits when the Player Core says you only get these benefits if a rule grants a devotee benefit?
results.reference_answer:
The deities section of the Starfinder Player Core explains that each deity lists benefits for their most ardent devotees and that you receive these benefits “only if you have a rule that specifically gives you a devotee benefit”【924521065135129†L52-L57】.  At present there is no Starfinder 2e class or option that grants the devotee benefit; the core classes like mystic provide access to divine spellcasting but not devotee benefits【924521065135129†L58-L60】.  Community discussion notes that the only way to gain a deity’s favored weapon is through a class that explicitly grants a devotee benefit, such as a cleric or champion imported from Pathfinder 2e or a future Starfinder class【292903967762728†L95-L100】.  Without such a rule, you can still gain proficiency with the favored weapon through ancestry or general feats, but you do not gain the devotee benefits themselves.

Because the Starfinder 2e Player Core currently provides no rule granting a devotee benefit, the question cannot be fully answered by the existing rules; gaining devotee benefits will require future options or GM permission.

results.query: For the envoy’s Get ’Em directive in Starfinder 2e, does the “Increase the damage by 1 at 5th, 10th, 15th, and 20th levels” clause apply to both the initial Strike when you Lead by Example and the subsequent damage, or only to the subsequent Strikes?
results.reference_answer: The envoy’s Get ’Em directive lets you spend one or two actions.  If you spend two actions, you Strike the target and add a status bonus to the damage roll equal to your Charisma modifier; regardless of whether that Strike hits, you and your allies gain a +1 status bonus to damage on subsequent Strikes made against the enemy until the start of your next turn, and the ability notes “Increase the damage by 1 at 5th, 10th, 15th, and 20th levels”【351438918869343†L245-L253】.  The rule distinguishes between the Charisma‑mod bonus on the initial Strike and the +1 status bonus on subsequent Strikes.  There is no indication that the level‑based damage increase modifies the Charisma‑based bonus on the initial Strike.  In contrast, the feat “Really Get ’Em!” explicitly changes the directive so that the bonus applies to “both the initial and subsequent Strikes”【791582213199924†L63-L65】, showing that the standard directive does not do so.  Therefore, the level‑based increase applies only to the +1 status bonus on subsequent Strikes, not to the initial Strike when you lead by example.

results.query: Does the multiple attack penalty (MAP) reduce the saving throw DC when using the Auto‑Fire or Area Fire actions in Starfinder 2e, or does it only affect your attack rolls?
results.reference_answer: The multiple attack penalty applies when you use an action with the attack trait more than once on your turn.  The rule states that your second attack takes a –5 penalty and any subsequent attacks take a –10 penalty, and it specifies that this penalty applies to “your check”【924928676747820†L63-L69】.  Every check with the attack trait counts toward the multiple attack penalty, including Strikes, spell attack rolls, and certain skill actions【924928676747820†L63-L69】.  The Auto‑Fire and Area Fire actions have the area and attack traits, but they don’t involve an attack roll; instead, each creature in the area makes a basic Reflex save against your class DC plus the weapon’s tracking value【537749994614211†L66-L72】【591629063671977†L66-L71】.  Because you aren’t making an attack roll, there is no check for the multiple attack penalty to modify, so the DC is unaffected.  However, using Auto‑Fire or Area Fire still has the attack trait, so it counts as an attack for purposes of increasing your multiple attack penalty on subsequent attack checks.

results.query: Does the Mystic’s Shadow connection concealment work against creatures with darkvision or greater darkvision in Starfinder 2e?
results.reference_answer: The Shadow connection’s harmony ability causes the target’s shadow to swell, reducing bright light to dim light and creating magical darkness that leaves the target concealed until the end of your next turn【277798940352199†L86-L93】.  Under the general lighting rules, creatures in dim light are concealed unless the observer has darkvision or low‑light vision or another precise sense【24788127418398†L66-L69】.  Darkvision allows a creature to see perfectly in darkness and dim light, but some magical darkness can still block normal darkvision, while greater darkvision can see through even those magical darkness effects【152005608778393†L64-L69】.  Because the Shadow connection creates magical darkness, creatures with normal darkvision still treat the target as concealed.  Only creatures with greater darkvision (or other senses that ignore magical darkness) would ignore the concealment.

Always calculate your multiple attack penalty based on  the weapon you're using on that attack, not ones you used  on previous attacks. For example, let's say you're wielding a  plasma sword in one hand and a battleglove (which has the  agile trait) in your other hand, and you make three Strikes  with these weapons over the course of your turn. The first  Strike you make during your turn has no penalty, no matter  what weapon you're using. The second Strike will take either  a –5 penalty if you use the plasma sword or a –4 penalty  if you use the battleglove. Your third attack would be a  –10 penalty with the plasma sword and a –8 penalty with  the battleglove, no matter what weapon you used for your  previous Strikes.

results.query: If a weapon has the Free‑hand trait, can you still use that hand to perform somatic or material components for spells in Starfinder 2e?
results.reference_answer: The free‑hand trait indicates that the weapon doesn’t occupy your hand.  A free‑hand weapon can’t be Disarmed, and you can use the hand covered by a free‑hand weapon to wield other items or perform manipulate actions【172801477619961†L55-L61】.  You can use abilities that require a free hand as long as you aren’t wielding anything else in that hand【172801477619961†L55-L61】.  Therefore, if you’re wearing a free‑hand weapon, you can still provide somatic or material components for spells with that hand, though you can’t attack with the free‑hand weapon while doing so.

results.query: Does attempting to Escape from a grab provoke a Reactive Strike in Starfinder 2e?

results.query: Can you use the Stand action to get up from prone while immobilized in Starfinder 2e?
